passages.explanation: The German painter attempts to paint Wanda as a Madonna, which Sacher-Masoch frames as comically naive idealism. He sees purity where Wanda embodies transgression. Severin recognizes the painter as a fellow 'donkey' because they share the same blindness: projecting their own fantasies onto a woman who will refuse every projection.

Wanda's derisive laughter at the Madonna concept is both cruel and honest. She refuses to be idealized in someone else's terms and insists on controlling her own image. The sound of her laughter drifting through the window while Severin listens below captures the spatial hierarchy of their relationship: she is always above, he always outside.

Wanda stages herself as Venus in Furs for the painter, insisting he capture the truth rather than a sanitized fiction. The painter goes 'terribly pale' because he recognizes a power that both attracts and terrifies him. Sacher-Masoch suggests that authentic feminine power is more compelling than any idealized version, and that the truth of desire is always darker than its polite representations.

The painter names his work 'Venus in Furs' and articulates the novel's central myth: the goddess of love descending into a cold modern world, wrapped in fur, with a slave at her feet. This is Sacher-Masoch's thesis stated through a character who understands it intellectually yet is being destroyed by it emotionally. Art becomes the medium for truths that cannot be safely lived.

During sittings, Wanda nibbles candies and bombards the painter with paper pellets, treating the creation of art as trivial amusement. She strikes Severin to summon the cruel expression the painting requires. Sacher-Masoch shows that cruelty is not only the subject of the artwork but its method of production: the painter needs real suffering to capture the image truthfully.

The painter begs to be whipped, and the contagion of masochism spreads from observer to participant. Witnessing submission awakens the same desire in him. Sacher-Masoch portrays cruelty as a performance that recruits its own audience into participation, blurring the line between spectator and victim.

Wanda ties the painter and whips him with evident pleasure. Severin, watching, feels a 'grim attraction' he cannot describe. The scene becomes a ritual with three participants: the wielder of power, the recipient, and the witness. Sacher-Masoch suggests that the erotic charge of dominance requires an audience to achieve its full intensity.

Severin, hidden behind a curtain, suffers the torment of imagination more than any whip could inflict. Unable to hear their whispered conversation, he fills the silence with his worst fears. Sacher-Masoch demonstrates that jealousy is the masochist's most potent drug: the agony of not knowing is more consuming than the pain of knowing.

The painter kneels before Wanda and begs for love; she responds only with the whip handle across his face. Her declaration that she has no heart but does have a whip distills the novel's central dynamic to its starkest form. The finished painting, infused with suffering, is described as almost supernaturally powerful, suggesting that unrequited passion produces art of terrifying authenticity.

The painter quietly tells Severin they both love the same woman, and calls her a 'demon' from German folklore. This brief moment of solidarity between rivals acknowledges a shared condition that neither can escape. His murmured folk reference suggests that Wanda represents something older and more universal than any individual woman.

The painter's secret copy of Wanda's portrait represents the only possession the powerless can claim: a private image of the one who owns them. He says he has 'earned it with his heart's blood,' equating artistic creation with sacrificial suffering. Sacher-Masoch implies that the masochist's truest relationship is not with the beloved but with his own pain, which he guards jealously.

Wanda dismisses the painter episode with feigned sympathy and immediately demands the carriage. Her extravagant new outfit modeled on Catherine the Great signals her full embrace of the despotic role. Sacher-Masoch links erotic power to aristocratic performance: the costume is not decoration but declaration of dominion.

The appearance of the Greek horseman introduces a man who is Severin's opposite: physically commanding, cruelly beautiful, self-possessed. Severin admits he 'understands the masculine Eros' and marvels at Socrates facing Alcibiades. Sacher-Masoch suggests that the masochist is drawn to the spectacle of power itself, regardless of who wields it. The Greek is not merely a rival but the embodiment of the dominance Severin has been worshipping in feminine form.
